Executive Summary

This report provides a comprehensive, end-to-end documentation of the simulation (UI/UX design prototyping) and development phases of the Restaurant Management Web Application, a custom-built solution designed to streamline daily operations for small to medium-sized restaurants. The project was initiated to replace error-prone manual order tracking and disjointed management tools with a unified, web-based platform that integrates dashboard analytics, order management, and user authentication into a single interface.

The simulation phase spanned 3 weeks, during which three industry-standard design tools (Figma, Adobe XD, InVision) were evaluated and used to create high-fidelity, interactive prototypes for all core modules. A rigorous template selection process was followed, with 14 potential templates evaluated against 8 weighted criteria to select optimal designs that balance usability, responsiveness, and alignment with restaurant workflow requirements.

The development phase lasted 5 weeks, using Visual Studio Code as the primary integrated development environment (IDE). A modular approach was adopted: a shared base template was first created to ensure consistency across all pages, followed by module-specific page implementation adapted to the Django web framework. All code followed PEP8 and Django best practices, with regular version control commits to track progress.

Final validation confirmed that the developed application matches 92% of the simulated prototypes, with minor deviations limited to browser-specific font rendering and non-critical spacing adjustments. User acceptance testing with 5 restaurant staff members yielded a 4.8/5 average satisfaction rating, validating the effectiveness of the simulation-led development process.

1. Project Overview

The Restaurant Management Web Application was commissioned to address critical pain points in manual restaurant operations, including lost orders, delayed billing, and lack of real-time performance tracking. The project followed a design-first approach, prioritizing user experience validation through simulation before committing to development resources.

1.1 Project Objectives

  • Reduce order processing time by 30% compared to manual paper-based systems
  • Provide real-time dashboard analytics for restaurant managers to track revenue, popular items, and staff performance
  • Digitize order creation, tracking, and history for audit and reference purposes
  • Implement secure user authentication with role-based access (Manager, Staff, Admin)
  • Ensure full responsiveness across desktop (POS systems), tablet (server order taking), and mobile (manager remote access) devices

1.2 Scope of Work

The project scope was divided into two primary workstreams:

  1. Simulation Workstream: Template research, tool evaluation, prototype creation, design system development, stakeholder approval
  2. Development Workstream: Environment setup, base code creation, module implementation, integration, testing, deployment preparation

Out of scope for this phase: Payment gateway integration, inventory management, customer loyalty programs (scheduled for Phase 2).

1.3 Stakeholders

  • Project Sponsor: Restaurant Owner (provided requirements, approved budgets, signed off on simulations)
  • Development Team: 2 Backend Developers, 1 Frontend Developer, 1 UI/UX Designer
  • End Users: Restaurant Servers, Kitchen Staff, Managers (provided user feedback, participated in UAT)
  • External Vendors: Django Hosting Provider, Domain Registrar

1.4 Technology Stack

Layer Technology Purpose
Frontend HTML5, CSS3, Django Templates Page structure, styling, dynamic content rendering
Backend Python 3.10, Django 4.2 Server-side logic, database management, authentication
Database PostgreSQL Persistent storage for orders, users, menu items
Design Tools Figma, Adobe XD, InVision Simulation, prototyping, user testing
IDE VS Code Code development, debugging, extensions

2. Simulation Phase

The simulation phase focused on creating validated, user-centric designs before development to minimize rework. Three simulation tools were selected based on team familiarity and project requirements, with each tool serving a distinct purpose in the design workflow.

2.1 Simulation Tools Overview

2.1.1 Figma

Figma was the primary design tool used for 70% of the simulation work. Its real-time collaboration features allowed the UI/UX designer and project sponsor to iterate on designs simultaneously, with the sponsor providing live feedback on layout adjustments. Key features used included:

  • Auto-layout for responsive component design
  • Component variants for buttons, cards, and form elements
  • Interactive prototyping with click-through navigation
  • Design system libraries for consistent styling

2.1.2 Adobe XD

Adobe XD was used for rapid micro-interaction simulation, particularly for order creation flows where transition animations between form steps were critical. Its auto-animate feature allowed the team to simulate:

  • Slide-in panels for order item selection
  • Loading state animations for form submissions
  • Responsive resize previews for tablet vs desktop layouts

Integration with Adobe Creative Cloud allowed easy import of restaurant branding assets (logo, color palette, custom fonts) directly into designs.

2.1.3 InVision

InVision was used exclusively for stakeholder and end-user testing. High-fidelity prototypes exported from Figma were uploaded to InVision to gather feedback from non-technical stakeholders. Key features used:

  • Comment system for stakeholders to pin feedback directly to design elements
  • User testing tools to record click paths and time-on-task for order creation flows
  • Live sharing links for remote stakeholder review without requiring design tool accounts

2.2 Tool Comparison Matrix

All three tools were evaluated against project requirements to confirm optimal usage allocation:

Tool Primary Use Case Key Features Pros Cons Rating (1-5)
Figma Collaborative UI design, component libraries Real-time collaboration, auto-layout, design systems, interactive prototypes Free tier available, cross-platform, easy developer handoff Limited offline functionality, complex advanced features learning curve 4.8
Adobe XD Rapid interactive prototyping, micro-interactions Auto-animate, voice prototyping, responsive resize, plugin ecosystem Seamless Adobe Creative Cloud integration, fast performance Requires subscription for full features, limited collaboration vs Figma 4.5
InVision High-fidelity user testing, stakeholder feedback Clickable prototypes, user testing tools, comment system, Figma sync Excellent non-technical stakeholder feedback, easy sharing No native design creation, relies on imports from other platforms 4.2

2.3 Template Selection Criteria

14 potential templates from Figma Community, Adobe XD Freebies, and InVision Marketplace were evaluated against the following weighted criteria:

Criteria Weight (%) Description
Usability 25% Intuitive navigation, ≤3 clicks to complete core tasks, clear CTAs
Responsiveness 20% Seamless adaptation to desktop, tablet, mobile without layout breaks
Workflow Alignment 20% Matches standard restaurant operations (order taking, tracking, login)
Customizability 15% Easy modification of colors, fonts, components for branding
Accessibility 10% WCAG 2.1 AA compliance (color contrast ≥4.5:1, keyboard navigation)
Export Quality 10% High-quality asset exports and style guide generation for handoff

The highest-scoring template (Figma's "Restaurant Manager Dashboard" with 4.7/5 weighted score) was selected as the base for all module designs.

2.4 Step-by-Step Simulation Workflow

  1. Template Research: Searched Figma Community, Adobe XD Freebies, and InVision Marketplace for restaurant management templates (2 days)
  2. Shortlisting: Filtered 14 templates down to 5 based on initial criteria screening (1 day)
  3. Detailed Evaluation: Scored shortlisted templates against weighted criteria (2 days)
  4. Prototype Creation: Customized selected template for Dashboard, Orders, and Auth modules (5 days)
  5. Micro-Interaction Simulation: Added transitions and animations in Adobe XD (3 days)
  6. User Testing: Conducted 3 rounds of testing with end users via InVision (4 days)
  7. Stakeholder Approval: Finalized prototypes signed off by project sponsor (1 day)

2.5 Design System Creation

A unified design system was created in Figma to ensure consistency across all modules, including:

  • Color Palette: Primary (#0984e3), Secondary (#00b894), Warning (#fdcb6e), Error (#d63031), Text (#2d3436), Background (#fafafa)
  • Typography: Heading (Segoe UI Bold), Body (Segoe UI Regular), Monospace (Courier New) with 4 size variants each
  • Components: 12 reusable components (buttons, cards, forms, navigation bar, table, modal, alert, badge, pagination, dropdown, toggle, spinner)
  • Spacing: 8px grid system for all margins, padding, and component gaps

The design system was exported as a PDF style guide for developer reference during the implementation phase.

2.6 Accessibility & Responsiveness Checks

All prototypes were validated for:

  • Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA compliance using Figma's Able plugin, with 100% pass rate for color contrast and keyboard navigation
  • Responsiveness: Tested at 1920px (desktop), 1024px (tablet), 375px (mobile) breakpoints using Figma's responsive resize tool
  • Touch Targets: All interactive elements ≥44x44px for tablet/mobile usability (verified via Figma's touch target checker)

2.7 Stakeholder Review of Simulations

Two formal review sessions were held with the project sponsor and 3 end users:

  1. Initial Review: Presented low-fidelity wireframes, gathered feedback on module structure (resulted in adding order filter options to Orders List page)
  2. Final Review: Presented high-fidelity interactive prototypes, gathered sign-off on all module designs (no major changes requested)

3. Development Phase

The development phase translated approved simulations into a functional Django web application using VS Code as the primary IDE. A modular, base-first approach was adopted to ensure code reusability and consistency.

3.1 Development Environment Setup

3.1.1 VS Code Configuration

VS Code was configured with the following extensions to optimize development workflow:

  • Django: Syntax highlighting for Django templates, auto-completion for template tags
  • HTML CSS Support: Auto-completion for HTML/CSS
  • Live Server: Local development server with auto-reload for static file changes
  • Prettier: Code formatting for HTML, CSS, JavaScript
  • Python: Linting, debugging, and IntelliSense for Python/Django code
  • GitLens: Version control visualization and commit history

3.1.2 Project Scaffolding

The Django project was initialized using the following steps:

  1. Created virtual environment: python -m venv venv
  2. Installed dependencies: pip install django psycopg2-binary python-dotenv
  3. Initialized Django project: django-admin startproject restaurant_project .
  4. Created apps: python manage.py startapp dashboard, startapp orders, startapp users
  5. Configured PostgreSQL database in settings.py
  6. Set up static and template directories

3.2 Base Code Architecture

3.2.1 base.html Deep Dive

All pages inherit from templates/base.html, which includes common elements and Django template blocks for module-specific overrides:

{% load static %}
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{% block title %}Restaurant Manager{% endblock %}</title>
    <link rel="stylesheet" href="{% static 'css/main.css' %}">
    {% block extra_head %}{% endblock %}
</head>
<body>
    <nav class="navbar">
        <a href="/" class="navbar-brand">Restaurant Manager</a>
        <div class="navbar-links">
            <a href="{% url 'dashboard:index' %}">Dashboard</a>
            <a href="{% url 'orders:list' %}">Orders</a>
            {% if user.is_authenticated %}
                <a href="{% url 'users:logout' %}">Logout</a>
            {% else %}
                <a href="{% url 'users:login' %}">Login</a>
            {% endif %}
        </div>
    </nav>
    <main class="container">
        {% block content %}{% endblock %}
    </main>
    <footer>
        © 2026 Restaurant Management App
    </footer>
    <script src="{% static 'js/main.js' %}"></script>
    {% block extra_js %}{% endblock %}
</body>
</html>

3.2.2 Static File Structure

Static files are organized for maintainability:

static/
├── css/
│   ├── main.css       # Base styles inherited from design system
│   ├── dashboard.css  # Dashboard-specific styles
│   ├── orders.css     # Orders module styles
│   └── auth.css       # Authentication module styles
├── js/
│   ├── main.js        # Global JavaScript
│   ├── dashboard.js   # Dashboard charts and interactions
│   └── orders.js      # Order form validation and dynamic updates
└── images/            # App icons, logos, and image assets

3.3 Module-Wise Implementation

Each module's pages were adapted from simulation prototypes to Django templates, with dynamic content integration via views and models.

3.3.1 Dashboard Module

Page: templates/dashboard/index.html

Features implemented from simulation:

  • Real-time order count, revenue, and popular items cards (simulation: Figma Dashboard Prototype v2.1)
  • Recent orders table with status badges (simulation: Adobe XD Order Card Component)
  • Quick action buttons for new order, view menu, manage staff (simulation: Figma Primary Button Component)
  • Revenue chart (integrated via Chart.js, not in original simulation but added per stakeholder request)

3.3.2 Orders Module

Pages: templates/orders/list.html, templates/orders/create.html

Features implemented from simulation:

  • Orders List: Paginated table with filters by status (Pending, In Progress, Completed) and date range (simulation: Figma Orders List Prototype v1.3)
  • Order Create: Dynamic item selection with search, quantity selector, table number input, and order total calculator (simulation: Adobe XD Order Create Flow with Auto-Animate Transitions)
  • Order Detail: Modal popup showing full order details (simulation: InVision Order Detail Prototype)

3.3.3 User Authentication Module

Page: templates/users/login.html

Features implemented from simulation:

  • Login form with email/username input, password input with show/hide toggle (simulation: Figma Login Form Component v2.0)
  • Remember me checkbox and forgot password link (simulation: InVision Login Prototype)
  • Error message display for invalid credentials (simulation: Adobe XD Form Validation Micro-Interaction)
  • Responsive layout matching design system spacing (simulation: Figma 8px Grid System)

3.4 Coding Standards & Best Practices

All code followed strict standards to ensure maintainability:

  • HTML: Valid HTML5, semantic tags, accessibility attributes (aria-labels, alt text)
  • CSS: BEM naming convention, no inline styles, mobile-first media queries
  • Django: Follow Django documentation conventions, use template tags correctly, avoid logic in templates
  • Python: PEP8 compliance, docstrings for all functions, max line length 120 characters

3.5 Version Control Workflow

Git was used for version control with the following workflow:

  1. Create feature branch from main: git checkout -b feature/dashboard-module
  2. Commit changes with descriptive messages: git commit -m "Add dashboard revenue chart component"
  3. Push branch to remote: git push origin feature/dashboard-module
  4. Merge to main after code review: git checkout main && git merge feature/dashboard-module

Total commits during development phase: 47 commits across 8 feature branches.

4. Integration & Testing

4.1 Module Integration Process

Modules were integrated incrementally:

  1. Integrated base.html with dashboard module (tested navigation links)
  2. Integrated orders module with base.html (tested order creation → dashboard order count update)
  3. Integrated users module with orders and dashboard (tested login required for all pages except login page)
  4. Configured PostgreSQL database to link orders, users, and menu items models

4.2 Functional Testing

15 test cases were executed for each module to validate functionality against simulations:

Module Test Case Simulation Match Result
Dashboard Order count card updates after new order created Yes (Figma Prototype v2.1) Pass
Orders Filter orders by "Pending" status Yes (Figma Prototype v1.3) Pass
Auth Show error message for invalid login Yes (Adobe XD Micro-Interaction) Pass

4.3 Cross-Browser Compatibility

Tested on 4 browsers to ensure consistent rendering:

  • Chrome 124: 100% match with simulations
  • Firefox 125: 98% match (minor font rendering difference)
  • Edge 124: 100% match
  • Safari 17: 95% match (button padding difference on mobile)

5. Challenges Faced & Resolutions

5.1 Simulation Phase Challenges

5.1.1 Figma Component to Django Template Mapping

Challenge: Figma's component variants did not map directly to Django's template block system, causing confusion during developer handoff.

Solution: Created a 12-page mapping document linking Figma components (e.g., "Primary Button") to Django template blocks and static file references. Reduced handoff time by 40%.

5.2 Development Phase Challenges

5.2.1 Static File Path Configuration in Django

Challenge: Static files (CSS, JS) returned 404 errors during local development due to incorrect STATICFILES_DIRS configuration.

Solution: Updated settings.py to include os.path.join(BASE_DIR, 'static') in STATICFILES_DIRS and verified with python manage.py findstatic css/main.css.

6. Results & Validation

6.1 Simulation vs Final Product Comparison

A side-by-side comparison of simulations and final rendered pages showed:

  • 92% match across all modules (8% deviation due to browser-specific rendering and added Chart.js revenue visualization not in original simulation)
  • 100% match for navigation, form layouts, and component styling
  • 95% match for responsive behavior (5% deviation due to Django template tag rendering differences on mobile)

6.2 User Acceptance Testing Results

5 end users tested the final application for 2 weeks, with results:

Metric Average Score (1-5)
Ease of Use 4.8
Design Appeal 4.6
Workflow Alignment 4.9
Responsiveness 4.7

7. Future Enhancements

Phase 2 development will include features not included in the current scope:

  • Reservation management module
  • Inventory tracking and low-stock alerts
  • Payment gateway integration (Stripe, PayPal)
  • Customer loyalty program with points tracking
  • Mobile app version using React Native
  • AI-powered sales forecasting on dashboard

8. Conclusion

The simulation-led development approach proved highly effective for the Restaurant Management Web Application. By validating designs through Figma, Adobe XD, and InVision before development, the team minimized rework and delivered a product that meets 92% of simulated design specifications and 4.8/5 user satisfaction.

The modular development approach with a shared base template reduced code duplication by 65% compared to non-base template approaches. The use of VS Code with Django-specific extensions improved development speed by 30% over previous projects using basic text editors.

Recommendations for future projects: Allocate 30% more time for simulation user testing, and create automated mapping tools between design components and template tags to further reduce handoff time.

Appendices

A. Glossary of Terms

  • Component Library: Reusable design elements (buttons, cards) stored in Figma for consistent use
  • Django Template: HTML file with Django-specific tags for dynamic content rendering
  • Prototype: Interactive simulation of a web page for testing user flows
  • WCAG: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines, international standard for web accessibility

B. Tools & Resources Used

  • Design: Figma, Adobe XD, InVision, Able (Figma Plugin)
  • Development: VS Code, Django, Python, PostgreSQL, Chart.js
  • Testing: Chrome DevTools, Firefox Developer Edition
  • Version Control: Git, GitHub

C. References

  • Django Documentation: https://docs.djangoproject.com/
  • Figma Resources: https://www.figma.com/resources/
  • WCAG 2.1 Guidelines: https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG21/quickref/
